title: Measuring Reconstruction Error
concept: Reconstruction loss quantifies how different the decoder's output is from the original input, guiding the network toward better compression.

exercises.prompt: When you photocopy a page, then photocopy the copy, then copy that copy... what usually happens?

options.text: The text gets a little blurrier or less accurate each time
The text becomes perfectly sharp forever

exercises.explain: Yes! Every time you copy a copy, small details get lost. That 'lost detail' is a lot like reconstruction error in AI!

cards.title: How Do We Know a Copy Is Bad?
cards.body: Imagine an AI squishes a picture of your cat down into a tiny code, then tries to rebuild the picture from that code. Sometimes the rebuilt cat looks perfect. Sometimes it looks like a blurry potato. How does the AI know which one it did? It measures the difference — and that difference has a name: reconstruction error!

cards.title: Reconstruction Loss
cards.body: Reconstruction loss (or reconstruction error) is a number that tells us how different the decoder's rebuilt output is from the original input. A small number means the rebuild looks almost the same as the original. A big number means the rebuild is way off. The network uses this number as a guide to get better at compressing and rebuilding over time.

cards.title: Like Grading a Drawing Copy
cards.body: Imagine your friend looks at a picture for 5 seconds, then tries to redraw it from memory. Afterward, you compare the two pictures side by side and count how many things are different — wrong colors, missing parts, wrong shapes. That 'difference count' is exactly what reconstruction loss does for an AI's rebuilt output.

cards.title: Two Rebuilt Pictures
cards.body: An autoencoder compresses a photo of a smiley face into a tiny code, then rebuilds it. Rebuild A looks almost identical to the original — low reconstruction error. Rebuild B is missing the smile and has the wrong color — high reconstruction error. The network prefers Rebuild A because its loss number is much smaller.

cards.title: Myth: Zero Error Means Perfect AI
cards.body: Myth: The goal is always to make reconstruction error exactly zero. Truth: A tiny bit of error is normal and even okay! If the error becomes exactly zero, the network might just be memorizing every input instead of learning a useful, general way to compress. A small, healthy error usually means the AI learned meaningful patterns.

cards.title: Comparing Two Rebuilds
cards.body: Reconstruction loss compares the original input to the output side by side.

left.text: Rebuilt image nearly matches the original — small loss number

right.text: Rebuilt image looks very different — large loss number

exercises.prompt: What does reconstruction loss actually measure?

options.text: How different the decoder's output is from the original input
How many layers the network has
How big the input image file is

exercises.explain: Reconstruction loss is a number showing the gap between the original input and the rebuilt output.

exercises.prompt: A high reconstruction loss means the rebuilt output looks very similar to the original input.
exercises.explain: False! A high reconstruction loss means the output is very different from the original — that's a bad rebuild.

exercises.sentence: A ____ reconstruction loss means the rebuilt output is very close to the original input.
exercises.explain: A small reconstruction loss means there's very little difference between the input and the rebuilt output — that's what we want!

exercises.prompt: An autoencoder rebuilds a picture of a dog, but the ears are missing and the fur color is wrong. What would you expect its reconstruction loss to be?

options.text: High, because the rebuild is quite different from the original
Zero, because it still looks like a dog

exercises.explain: Missing ears and wrong colors mean the rebuild differs a lot from the original, so the reconstruction loss would be high.

exercises.prompt: An autoencoder is trained to compress and rebuild handwritten digit images. After training, which situation shows the network is doing a GOOD job, based on reconstruction loss?

options.text: The rebuilt digits look nearly identical to the originals, giving a low reconstruction loss
The rebuilt digits are random squiggles, giving a high reconstruction loss
The network takes a long time to run, regardless of image quality
The compressed code is very large in file size

exercises.explain: A good autoencoder produces rebuilds that closely match the originals, which shows up as a low reconstruction loss — that's the real sign of success!
